expectations.text: Identifies that pure-FP Haskell has no runtime object identity — equal values are indistinguishable — and flags this as a problem the integrator must solve, not assume away
expectations.evidence: Phase 1 row 2 and Section 2.1: 'Pure value semantics; no stable runtime identity... Haskell has no id().' Explicitly distinguishes from Python's id() and frames identity choice as load-bearing.

expectations.text: Names at least one concrete strategy for supplying identity in Haskell: hash-consing, structural hash with cycle detection, path-from-root, explicit ID field, or System.Mem.StableName / observable sharing
expectations.evidence: Section 2.1 'Identity strategy': two named alternatives — (A) Structural hash with explicit hash function, recommended as default; (B) Path identity. StableName named as third option but rejected as 'too brittle for AST visualization.'

expectations.text: Picks a Haskell-idiomatic spec-attachment seam: type-class instances (e.g. class Spytial a), Generic deriving, or a separate Spec ADT — and contrasts with non-natural choices
expectations.evidence: Section 2.2: 'Seam: a SpytialSpec type class, one instance per type, composing automatically.' Concrete Haskell signature 'class SpytialSpec a where spec :: Proxy a -> [SpecFragment]' with example instances. Phase 1 row 3 justifies it: 'Type classes are the canonical Haskell seam for attach metadata to a type.'

expectations.text: Names AST as the classical example for Subproblem 4 (Gaps) — the user explicitly said AST trees, so the design must engage with it concretely
expectations.evidence: Whole design centered on a 'Recipe AST' with constructors Recipe/Step/Mix/Bake/Wait/Ingredient. Section 2.4 addresses gaps for that AST specifically.

expectations.text: Addresses ordering of child lists (insertion order, position-as-relation, or orderBy directive) — important for ASTs whose children have semantic positions
expectations.evidence: Section 2.4: 'AST children are ordered (the order of arguments to a constructor matters)... add an orderBy via the projection directive on list-type atoms so the layout solver respects it. Don't rely on Spytial inferring order from tuple order alone.'

expectations.text: States a path to emit JSON conforming to JSONDataInstance, with Aeson or hand-rolled serialization
expectations.evidence: Section 2.1 final paragraph: 'You'll emit this via Path A — go through JSONDataInstance rather than implementing IDataInstance natively.' Phase 4 names aeson-style ToJSON instances.

expectations.text: Acknowledges the user is a beginner — is welcoming, doesn't dump 4 phases of jargon without scaffolding, and offers a simple first step
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'Welcome' but immediately dumps 237 lines of phased design doc using the Phase-1-through-Phase-4 framework, plus all four 'Subproblem' labels. The user explicitly said 'i've never integrated anything with spytial before.' A beginner-first response would skip the 4-phase apparatus entirely on the first pass and lead with one concrete code example. The skill's structure leaks through despite the opening welcome.

expectations.text: Provides a path to a minimum-viable build (JSON emitter + HTML harness rendering one AST example), not just abstract design
expectations.evidence: Phase 4 enumerates a five-module Cabal package, the order to write the modules, a round-trip test for the recipe AST, and an explicit 'Then stop' gate.

expectations.text: Skill does NOT engage its phased walkthrough — response does not contain 'Phase 1' / 'Phase 2' / 'four subproblems' / 'recover/attach/present/gaps' framework
expectations.evidence: Response has no Phase headers, no mention of the four subproblems framework. Transcript explicitly states 'Did NOT invoke the integrate-language skill.' The agent read the skill's frontmatter, recognized the user is a downstream consumer of spytial-py asking a usage question, and answered directly.

expectations.text: Response is concise — under ~3000 characters / ~500 words. The user asked a focused question, not a design question

expectations.text: Discusses orientation as a layout concept — either the orientation directive/decorator in spytial-py or reversing the relation in the data model
expectations.evidence: Both options discussed: Option 1 reverses the relation (parent owns children), Option 2 uses @orientation and @hide_field decorators. Notes the distinction between layout direction and arrowhead direction.

expectations.text: Does not interrogate the user with the five Phase 1 questions about identity model / spec idiom / surface / target user

expectations.text: Provides at least one concrete code-shaped suggestion: a decorator like @spytial.orientation(...), a YAML directive, or a code snippet — actionable, not just abstract

expectations.text: Names a specific relationalizer technique (procedural macros, derive macros, or compile-time type-directed serialization) and ties it to the Rust type system
expectations.evidence: Section 2.1: 'Technique: type-directed serialization via a derive macro, traversal at runtime, JSON output.' Justifies against Rust's no-runtime-reflection model.

expectations.text: Addresses identity for Rust types — distinguishes value semantics from references (&, Box, Rc, Arc), or notes that struct field paths can serve as stable IDs
expectations.evidence: Phase 1 row 2: 'identity = address of the value during traversal (&T as *const T as usize)'. Section 2.1: 'Rc<T> and Box<T> — delegate identity to the pointee, i.e., intern((*self).as_ptr() as usize, ...)'.

expectations.text: Drafts at least one concrete macro/attribute signature, e.g. #[orientation(...)] / #[align(...)] / #[group(...)] / #[derive(SpytialDecorators)]
expectations.evidence: Section 2.2 has #[derive(Spytial)], #[spytial(orientation(selector=..., directions=[...]))], #[spytial(group(field=..., group_on=..., add_to_group=...))], #[spytial(atom_color(...))].

expectations.text: Recommends emitting JSON conforming to JSONDataInstance (Path A) over implementing IDataInstance directly (Path B)
expectations.evidence: Section 2.1 final paragraph: 'JSON path vs. IDataInstance: emit JSON, wrap with JSONDataInstance. The custom-data-instance docs are explicit that this is what every existing integration does. Only deviate if you measure a problem.'

expectations.text: Addresses Subproblem 4 (Gaps) for at least one of the user's examples — e.g. binary tree height as a derived metric, Vec ordering, or sharing of Person via parent pointer
expectations.evidence: Section 2.4 covers all four: Vec ordering via index relation, BinTree ordering via orientation, BinTree height as derived metric computed in relationalizer, Option<Rc<Person>> sharing via intern cache + cycle handling.

notes: Skill produces +25pp pass-rate improvement (96% vs 71%) AND uses 35s less time and ~7800 fewer tokens per run on average. Counterintuitive — skills typically add overhead. Likely cause: without the skill, agents waste tool calls on WebSearch trying to discover what Spytial is, and end up at spytial-py instead of spytial-core's integration docs. With the skill, the agent fetches a curated set of canonical URLs and is on-task immediately.

eval-haskell-forward shows the largest delta (with_skill 87.5% vs without_skill 37.5%, +50pp). The without_skill agent fundamentally misunderstood the task: it discovered only spytial-py and proposed bridging Haskell -> JSON -> Python instead of building a native integration. The skill prevents this by directly pointing the agent at the integration recipe and the IDataInstance contract. This is the highest-leverage case — fresh hosts where the agent has weak priors.

eval-rust-backtest shows a smaller delta (with_skill 100% vs without_skill 75%, +25pp). The without_skill agent did better here because (a) Rust integration patterns are more discoverable from spytial-py docs and (b) procedural-macro idioms are a well-trodden path. Two specific failures without the skill: never names JSONDataInstance / Path A vs Path B, and never references Caraspace as precedent. Both gaps would surface during implementation as wasted effort.

eval-near-miss-graph: both configurations score 100%. The skill correctly did NOT trigger on a downstream-consumer question (the agent read the skill frontmatter, recognized scope mismatch, and answered directly without engaging the phased walkthrough). This validates that the skill's description is well-bounded — it doesn't over-trigger.

Single discriminating failure for with_skill: eval-haskell-forward beginner-friendliness. The user said 'i've never integrated anything with spytial before' but received 237 lines of phased design doc with all the Phase-1-through-Phase-4 apparatus. Suggests the skill could carry a scaffolding mode for beginners (e.g. 'lead with one runnable example, layer the four-subproblem framing in only after rapport is established').

Variance: with_skill stddev on pass-rate is 7%, without_skill is 31% — without the skill, results swing wildly depending on what the agent stumbles on during research. The skill is a strong variance reducer in addition to a quality lifter.

All three 'beginner-friendliness' style assertions could be made more discriminating. Currently the assertion fires on the *opening tone* but the skill agent passes despite producing a 200+ line design document — the assertion should additionally check 'response is under N words on first turn for first-time-user prompts' or similar.
